text: The surgeon prepares to inflate the abdomen for the procedure, discussing trocar placement along the costal margin, with adjustments based on the patient's body habitus.
The surgeon identifies the eleventh and twelfth ribs and locates the rectus muscle. A visible scar is noted in the suprapubic region, likely from a previous surgical procedure.

The surgical team adjusts the operating table positioning, turning it slightly towards them. The setup includes four robotic ports and one assistant port, utilizing an air seal port for larger instruments, requiring a twelve millimeter size.

The surgeon uses an air seal trocar for suctioning, noting the patient's eleventh rib is depressed towards the abdomen, potentially obscuring the upper pole of the kidney and adrenal gland. The surgical team begins dissection of the descending colon, spleen, and pancreas to locate the renal vein.

The surgeon rolls the surrounding tissues away to expose the upper pole of the kidney and the adrenal gland. The dissection proceeds to locate the renal vein. The patient exhibits vascular abnormalities, with a short and wide adrenal vein branching into two horns, which may require separate or combined management based on the dissection findings.

The surgeon identifies a branch of the renal artery supplying the adrenal gland, preparing to clip and cut it to prevent vascular dissemination during adrenal manipulation. The renal vein is dissected and clamped to control catecholamine release and avoid hypertension.

The surgeon identifies congenital adhesions between the colon, spleen, and the posterior upper part of the kidney. Using dissection techniques, the surgeon takes down these ligamentous structures to medialize the colon, aiming for a wide exposure of the surgical field. The splenicolic ligament is visible, with the spleen seen at a distance, positioned high up.

The surgeon carefully dissects through the abdominal cavity, isolating the colon and the upper pole of the kidney. Despite the patient's imperfect body habitus, the surgeon navigates the depths of the abdomen with caution, ensuring clear visibility of the anatomical structures.

The surgeon mobilizes the colon to drop it, creating space to improve visibility of the kidney hilum and renal vein. The kidney is exposed with minimal surrounding fat tissue, indicating the patient has little adipose tissue in this area.
The surgeon identifies the colon and maintains a distance from it while separating the spleen from the accessory splenic tissue.

The surgeon identifies the spleen and a round structure that may be an extension of the spleen. A segment of Gerota's fascia is left intact to serve as a protective buttress for the delicate splenic tissue.
The pancreas is visible in the surgical field as the camera view shifts.

The surgeon carefully dissects near the end of the spleen, ensuring the stomach does not slide down into the surgical field. An NG tube is maintained in the stomach to prevent accidental injury during dissection.
The surgeon visualizes the adrenal mass and attempts to rotate the spleen to free it from its attachments, aiming to move it away from the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies the meso and the mass, focusing on mobilizing the colon medially to create more space for accessing the hilum. The colon is visibly positioned in front of the surgical field, requiring careful manipulation.
The surgeon identifies the duodenum in the surgical field and notes the approximate location of the hilum, while creating space for further dissection.
The surgeon identifies the large splenic artery, which is visible and runs towards the spleen, marking the upper boundary of the dissection area.
The surgeon carefully maintains a layer of Gerota's fascia around the adrenal gland to protect vital structures during dissection. The search for the renal vein continues in the surrounding area.

The surgeon attempts to define anatomical structures, suggesting the identification of the gonadal vein to follow it cranially. The forearm is used as a traction device, and there is further mobilization of the colon.
The surgeon uses a forearm device to elevate and move the kidney, employing the TIRTI-UP technique to manipulate the organ within the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully advances towards the renal hilum, aiming to locate the renal vein amidst surrounding structures.

The surgeon identifies the renal vein amidst vascular abnormalities around the renal hilum and adrenal gland. The renal vein is visible, and the surgeon proceeds with further dissection. The adrenal mass is also visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon attempts to locate the adrenal vein using scissors, noting its broad and short origin before bifurcation.
The surgeon identifies a vessel that appears to be the adrenal vein, noting its unexpected length. The surgical field is adjusted to create more room around the vein.

The surgeon identifies the gonadal vein descending and the renal vein in the surgical field. On the medial side of the adrenal gland, lymphatic tissue is visible, and gentle downward pressure is applied to expose the area further.

The surgeon identifies the adrenal vein, noting its length is greater than anticipated based on preoperative imaging. The vein is exposed, but there is uncertainty about accessing the area beneath it.

The surgeon identifies the adrenal vein, noting adhesions along its path. Careful manipulation is required due to the potential presence of large vessels, despite the adrenal vein typically being the largest single vein in the area. A small branch of the vein is visible.
The surgeon applies Hemlock clips to secure vessels near the renal wing, adjusting the orientation of the clips with the belly facing upwards.
The surgeon observes a large renal vein with no visible bifurcation, adjusting the position slightly to improve visibility.
The surgeon begins to separate the adrenal gland from the upper pole of the kidney, identifying a renal artery branch in the process.
The surgeon encounters difficulty maneuvering scissors near the splenic artery, which is visually close to the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the upper pole of the kidney and begins to tackle the adrenal gland from this side, searching for a suitable dissection plane to proceed with the separation.
The surgeon identifies the kidney and begins mobilizing the perirenal fat along with the adrenal gland, ensuring a safe distance from the mass. The fat appears sticky during manipulation.

The surgeon identifies small bleeders within the fat between the kidney and adrenal gland, using clips or cautery to manage them. The upper part of the kidney is visible, with adhesions present, suggesting a vessel may be embedded within the tissue.
The surgeon identifies the renal vein and carefully releases the mass from its proximity, using suction to clear the area. A small clip is applied below the bipolar instrument to secure the tissue.
The surgeon identifies the renal artery, visible in the surgical field, and examines its position relative to the surrounding structures.
The surgeon identifies a branch of the renal artery extending towards the adrenal gland, noting its proximity. The team discusses the availability of small or medium clips for securing the vessel.
The surgeon applies a clip to a branch near the renal artery using a clip-applier, followed by suction to clear the surgical field.

The surgeon attempts to reach the psoas muscle, aiming to identify the posterior surface. The complex vascular anatomy is visible, with a branch of the renal artery running nearby. The renal vein, previously clipped, is noted to be relatively large.
The surgeon reaches the surface of the kidney and performs further dissection in the area, requesting assistance to gently lift the screen for better visibility.
The surgeon mobilizes the inferior and medial aspects of the adrenal gland, preparing to isolate it from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon identifies small arteries around the adrenal gland, applying clips to prevent bleeding. The adrenal gland is carefully maneuvered to respect surrounding vascular structures, ensuring minimal disruption.

The surgeon identifies small vessels near the adrenal gland and applies a medium clip to secure them, demonstrating a strategic approach by addressing easier areas before moving to more challenging sections.

The surgeon identifies small lymphatic and vascular structures near the adrenal gland that need careful management. The dissection progresses towards the psoas muscle, with the surgeon cutting through the surrounding fat tissue in the absence of large vessels.

The surgeon identifies lymphatic structures near the adrenal gland, noting the presence of an artery and vein extending from the Achilles side to the adrenal side. Careful consideration is given to preserving these lymphatics, although removal may be necessary.

The surgeon attempts to use the fourth arm of the robotic system but finds it obstructive. The surgical field includes vessels near the pancreas, requiring careful handling. The surgeon prepares the site and places a medium-sized clip on a vessel.
The surgeon identifies large vessels in the surgical field and prepares to secure them using two large clips, placing one below and one above the vessels.

The surgeon approaches from the top and carefully maneuvers downwards, handling the vessels with precision. Additional adjustments are made to ensure adequate control over the vessel size, with the surgeon deciding to take another vessel for better management.
The lateral side of the adrenal gland appears almost clean, indicating that dissection and tissue clearance have been effectively performed in this area.
The surgeon uses a suction device to clear the surgical field, removing fluids and debris to improve visibility.
The surgeon identifies medium-sized vessels in the surgical field and applies a clip to secure them, followed by suction to clear the area.
The surgeon follows the contours of the truss muscle, while periodically cleaning the lens to maintain clear visibility of the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies a small vessel and uses cauterization to prevent bleeding, ensuring there is no residual bleeding in the surgical field. A larger vessel is noted, and the surgeon prepares to address it.
The surgical field shows the spleen and its artery. The surgeon carefully manages the area, ensuring minimal blood loss.
The surgeon attempts to maneuver around a sizable adrenal mass using scissors, encountering resistance that requires additional force to navigate the tissue.
The surgeon attempts to lift the rib cage, requesting assistance to adjust the position towards one side while carefully avoiding contact with the spleen using the instrument tip.
The surgeon encounters a tough bleeder from the side and considers taking action to address it, while another team member is positioned in front of the surgeon.

The surgeon identifies a large artery among the vascular structures, deciding to preserve it until confirming its connection to the adrenal gland. Another artery is noted, and the surgeon instructs an assistant to take it.
The surgeon identifies the renal artery and renal vein, pointing them out on the screen for visualization.

The surgeon identifies the healthy part of the adrenal gland, considering the possibility of a partial adrenalectomy. The spleen is lifted slightly to improve access, though the scissors are moving with difficulty due to spatial constraints.
